title: RIP bargain bin: The price impact of Sony's disc-free PlayStation plan
gist: An Ars analysis of 19 top PlayStation games found used discs were the cheapest option for most games most of the year, and that Sony's plan to end physical PlayStation disc production in 2028 will remove the competitive 'release valve' that currently pressures the PlayStation Store into deep periodic digital discounts.

themes.theme: Digital 'Standard' price as an artificial anchor

insights.claim: The 'Standard' PlayStation Store digital price was the most expensive option for 15 of 19 top games analyzed, with six-to-eight-year-old titles like Call of Duty: Modern Warfare and Assassin's Creed Odyssey still listed at their $59.99 launch price while used discs sold for $9.99 at GameStop.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: The inflated standard price is a marketing device, not a real price—useful lens on how digital storefronts manufacture discount perception.
insights.quote: That digital price was the most expensive option available for 15 of the 19 games we looked at
insights.locator: A quick glance at the results shows

insights.claim: High 'Standard' prices on old PlayStation Store games are an artificial level that lets publishers periodically post a splashy '80% off' banner, since most customers have been trained to wait for a sale.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Reveals the psychological pricing playbook of digital storefronts—relevant to any subscription/marketplace monetization model.
insights.quote: the high “Standard” price on offer for many older PlayStation Store games is simply an artificial level that lets the publisher briefly post a splashy “80% off” banner every few months.

themes.theme: Used discs as the competitive release valve

insights.claim: Even after deep digital discounts of 40-80%, a used disc remained the cheapest option for seven of 19 games, with Dark Souls III savings of $10 versus its $30 PlayStation Store 'sale' price.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Quantifies the ongoing discount value physical resale provides—the exact value that disappears in a disc-free model.
insights.locator: But waiting for a PlayStation Store sale isn’t always a great deal

insights.claim: Digital discount prices were only offered between 13% and 33% of the time for the bestsellers analyzed, sometimes requiring buyers to wait multiple months for a weekslong sale.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Digital deals are time-gated and require vigilance, whereas cheap discs are always available—an underrated consumer-surplus difference.
insights.quote: those discount prices on downloads were only offered between 13 and 33 percent of the time.
insights.locator: While a cheap disc can be purchased pretty much whenever you want

insights.claim: Cheap used discs currently act as a release valve for pricing pressure, setting a competitive floor that digital sale pricing must take into account.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Removing the physical alternative removes the external price constraint—the core mechanism behind the disc-free pricing thesis.
insights.quote: cheap disc games exist as a sort of release valve for pricing pressure, setting a competitive price that digital sale pricing has to at least take into account as an alternative.
insights.locator: Right now, cheap disc games exist as a sort of release valve

themes.theme: Disc-free future: monopoly vs Steam counter-evidence

insights.claim: Ending PlayStation disc production in 2028 also eliminates the used-disc resale market, removing the de facto discount players get from reselling completed games.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Loss of a resale market is a hidden cost transfer from consumers to publishers—directionally margin-positive for Sony and publishers.
insights.locator: Remember, too, that buying a game on disc means you can resell it

insights.claim: A PlayStation Store distribution monopoly won't necessarily force high prices, since PC gaming's lack of discs still produced a Steam 'race to the bottom' in digital pricing plus frequent deep sales.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: The Steam precedent tempers the bearish-for-consumers thesis; distribution monopoly does not automatically equal pricing power.
insights.quote: the lack of disc-based options for most games hasn’t stopped a “race to the bottom” digital game pricing effect from developing on platforms like Steam
insights.locator: However, a PlayStation Store monopoly on game distribution

insights.claim: Without used discs, publishers may have less impetus to offer the deep downloadable discounts currently common on the PlayStation Store.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: The key uncertainty for post-2028 consumer pricing—whether discount depth persists absent physical competition.
insights.locator: If used discs are simply not available for future PlayStation games
